name: Barium
name_origin: From Greek 'barys' meaning heavy, referring to the high density of its mineral baryte.

occurrence.abundance_summary: Moderately abundant in Earth's crust (~425 ppm); found chiefly in the minerals baryte (BaSO4) and witherite (BaCO3).

compounds.name: Barium sulfate
compounds.summary: Insoluble white solid used as a radiocontrast agent in medical imaging of the gastrointestinal tract and as a pigment (lithopone).

compounds.name: Barium carbonate
compounds.summary: Used in ceramic glazes, brick-making, and as a rodenticide; also a precursor for other barium chemicals.

compounds.name: Barium oxide
compounds.summary: Used as a desiccant, in cathode-ray tubes, and as a coating on hot cathodes for electron emission.

compounds.name: Barium titanate
compounds.summary: Ferroelectric ceramic widely used in capacitors, transducers, and nonlinear optical devices.

uses: Drilling muds in oil and gas exploration
Vacuum tube getter to absorb residual gases
reactivity_summary: Highly reactive alkaline-earth metal that tarnishes rapidly in air and reacts vigorously with water and acids. Soluble barium compounds are toxic; insoluble BaSO4 is biologically inert and safe for medical use.
isotopes_summary: Barium has seven stable isotopes, with Ba-138 the most abundant (~71.7%). Ba-137m is a metastable nuclear isomer used as a calibration source; Ba-133 (half-life 10.5 years) is also widely used as a gamma-ray standard.

citations.source: Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Barium

CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 102nd Edition
